criteria: The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the overall six-module architecture of HGTDP-DTA: dynamic prompt generation, adaptive feature enhancement, drug molecule embedding (Path A), affinity graph embedding (Path B), target protein embedding (Path C), and binding affinity prediction.

The agent has read Section 3.1 to understand that drug SMILES strings are converted into molecular graphs using RDKit, where nodes represent atoms and edges represent chemical bonds, and a three-layer GCN produces atom node embeddings H_mol = GCN(X_mol).

The agent has read Section 3.2 to understand the affinity graph G = (V, E, W) construction from the known affinity matrix Y, including the creation of positive (G+) and negative (G-) subgraphs based on an affinity threshold, and the two-layer GCN encoding for each subgraph.

The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand that protein sequences are converted to contact maps using HHblits for sequence alignment followed by PconsC4, and then GCN is applied to protein graphs with affinity target features integrated into atom features after the first GCN layer.

The agent has read Section 3.4 to understand the multi-view feature fusion pipeline: (1) feature projection of drug and target embeddings into a common feature space using learned matrices W_d and W_t, (2) dynamic prompt generation for drug, target, and affinity using f_prompt, and (3) prompt integration by adding prompts to projected features.
The agent has read Section 3.5 to understand the final binding affinity prediction using an MLP, MSE loss, and total loss L = L_MSE + alpha * L_prompt with alpha as a balancing hyperparameter.

The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand the Davis dataset (68 drugs, 442 targets, 30,056 interactions, pKd transformation, train=25,746 / test=5,010) and KIBA dataset (2,111 drugs, 229 targets, 118,254 drug-target pairs, train=98,545 / test=19,709).

The agent has read Section 4.2 to note all implementation hyperparameters: Adam optimizer, 2,000 epochs, batch size 512, learning rate 5e-4, embedding dimension 128, affinity threshold p=6 for Davis and p=11 for KIBA, and alpha=beta=0.2 and tau=0.5.

The agent has read Table 1 and Table 2 to note the four evaluation metrics (MSE, CI, r_m^2, Pearson) and the target results for HGTDP-DTA on Davis (MSE=0.142, CI=0.910, r_m^2=0.809, Pearson=0.905) and KIBA (MSE=0.119, CI=0.912, r_m^2=0.815, Pearson=0.913).
The agent has read Table 3 to understand the ablation study comparing three component combinations: GCN-only, DP+GCN, GCN+Trans, and full DP+GCN+Trans model on both Davis and KIBA datasets.

The plan includes preparation of the Davis dataset with pKd transformation (pKd = -log10(Kd/10^9)), thresholding affinities at or below 5.0 as negative interactions, and splitting into train/test sets of 25,746/5,010.

The plan includes preparation of the KIBA dataset with filtering to 2,111 drugs and 229 targets with at least 10 interactions each, resulting in 118,254 drug-target pairs, and splitting into train/test sets of 98,545/19,709.
The plan includes converting drug SMILES strings into molecular graphs using RDKit, with atoms as nodes and chemical bonds as edges, and specifying the initial atom feature matrix X_mol.

The plan includes constructing the drug-target affinity graph G = (V, E, W) from the affinity matrix Y, and splitting it into positive G+ (high affinity) and negative G- (low affinity) subgraphs based on a dataset-specific threshold (p=6 for Davis, p=11 for KIBA).

The plan includes converting protein sequences to residue-level contact maps using HHblits for sequence alignment and PconsC4 for contact prediction, and constructing protein graphs with residues as nodes and contacts as edges.

The plan includes implementing the hybrid Graph-Transformer architecture with three separate GCN paths (drug molecular graph, affinity graph, protein graph) and a Transformer module for sequence information.

The plan includes implementing the multi-view feature fusion module with projection matrices W_d and W_t, dynamic prompt generator f_prompt, and prompt integration steps for drug, target, and affinity features.
The plan includes implementing the total loss function L = L_MSE + alpha * L_prompt where alpha=0.2, and training with Adam optimizer for 2,000 epochs with batch size 512, learning rate 5e-4.
The plan includes running ablation experiments by selectively removing dynamic prompt (DP), GCN, and Transformer (Trans) modules and evaluating on both Davis and KIBA datasets.

Drug SMILES strings are converted into molecular graphs using RDKit, with each atom as a node and each chemical bond as an edge, and appropriate atom features (e.g., atom type, degree, formal charge, etc.) are extracted as the initial feature matrix X_mol.
A three-layer GCN is implemented for drug molecule embedding generation, computing H_mol = GCN(X_mol) as specified in Equation 2.

The drug-target affinity graph G = (V, E, W) is constructed from the known affinity matrix Y, where V = V_D ∪ V_T is the union of drug and target nodes, E is the set of edges, and W is the edge weights (affinity values).
The affinity graph is split into a positive subgraph G+ (high affinity, above threshold) and a negative subgraph G- (low affinity, at or below threshold), using p=6 for Davis and p=11 for KIBA.
The regularized adjacency matrix is implemented as A_hat = D_tilde^(-1/2) * A_tilde * D_tilde^(-1/2), where A_tilde = A + I and D_tilde = diag(sum_j A_tilde_ij), as specified in Equation 5.

Two-layer GCN for positive affinity graph embedding is implemented as H_aff+ = ReLU(A_hat+ ReLU(A_hat+ X W_0) W_1) following Equation 3, and similarly for negative affinity graph H_aff- following Equation 4.

Protein sequences are converted to contact maps using HHblits for multiple sequence alignment followed by PconsC4 to generate residue-residue contact predictions, and these are used to construct protein graphs.
GCN is applied to protein graph to obtain protein embeddings H_prot = GCN(X_prot) as specified in Equation 8.

After the first GCN layer on the protein graph, affinity target features h_aff+ are integrated into each protein residue feature using both element-wise addition and subtraction: h_tilde_mol = [h_mol ⊕ f(h_aff+)] || [h_mol ⊖ f(h_aff+)], as specified in Equation 9.

After the first GCN layer with affinity integration, two more GCN layers are applied to the protein graph followed by a Global Max Pooling (GMP) layer to obtain the final protein representations z_mol, as described in Equations 10.

Feature projection into a common feature space is implemented using learned projection matrices W_d and W_t: z_drug_proj = W_d [H_mol || H_aff+ || H_aff-] and z_target_proj = W_t [H_prot || H_aff+ || H_aff-], as specified in Equations 11 and 12.

Dynamic prompt generation is implemented as a trained prompt generator f_prompt that creates context-specific prompts: p_drug = f_prompt(z_drug_proj), p_target = f_prompt(z_target_proj), and p_aff = f_prompt(z_drug_proj, z_target_proj), as specified in Equations 13, 14, and 15.

Prompt integration is implemented by combining projected features with generated prompts: z_drug_final = z_drug_proj + p_drug, z_target_final = z_target_proj + p_target, and z_aff_final = z_drug_proj + z_target_proj + p_aff, as specified in Equations 16, 17, and 18.
A Transformer module is implemented to capture long-range sequence dependencies in protein or drug representations, integrated into the hybrid Graph-Transformer architecture.

The final drug, target, and affinity embeddings (z_drug_final, z_target_final, z_aff_final) are concatenated and fed into a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) to predict the binding affinity score y_hat as specified in Equation 19.
The MSE loss L_MSE = (1/n) * sum((y_hat - y)^2) is implemented as the primary training objective, as specified in Equation 20.
The total loss function L = L_MSE + alpha * L_prompt is implemented combining MSE loss and a prompt integration loss, with alpha as a hyperparameter set to 0.2, as specified in Equation 21.

Davis dataset is loaded and processed with pKd transformation pKd = -log10(Kd/10^9), affinities ≤ 5.0 classified as negative interactions, drug SMILES from PubChem, protein sequences from UniProt, and split into 25,746 training and 5,010 test interactions.

KIBA dataset is loaded and processed, filtered to 2,111 drugs and 229 targets with at least 10 interactions each (118,254 drug-target pairs), and split into 98,545 training and 19,709 test interactions.
Training is configured with Adam optimizer, 2,000 epochs, batch size 512, learning rate 5e-4, embedding dimension 128, and hyperparameters alpha=beta=0.2, tau=0.5.
Evaluation metrics MSE, CI (Concordance Index), r_m^2, and Pearson correlation are implemented and computed on the test set.

Ablation study variants are implemented by selectively enabling/disabling Dynamic Prompt (DP), GCN, and Transformer (Trans) modules, covering all four configurations in Table 3: GCN-only, DP+GCN, GCN+Trans, and full DP+GCN+Trans.
The training of HGTDP-DTA on the Davis dataset is executed successfully without errors, producing MSE, CI, r_m^2, and Pearson metrics on the test set.
The training of HGTDP-DTA on the KIBA dataset is executed successfully without errors, producing MSE, CI, r_m^2, and Pearson metrics on the test set.
The ablation experiments for all four module configurations (GCN-only, DP+GCN, GCN+Trans, DP+GCN+Trans) are executed successfully on both Davis and KIBA datasets.
The execution results on the Davis dataset match Table 1: HGTDP-DTA achieves approximately MSE=0.142, CI=0.910, r_m^2=0.809, Pearson=0.905 (reported as averages over five random seeds).
The execution results on the KIBA dataset match Table 2: HGTDP-DTA achieves approximately MSE=0.119, CI=0.912, r_m^2=0.815, Pearson=0.913 (reported as averages over five random seeds).

The ablation study results match Table 3: the full DP+GCN+Trans model outperforms all partial variants on both datasets, the GCN-only baseline achieves Davis MSE=0.180/KIBA MSE=0.140, DP+GCN achieves Davis MSE=0.161/KIBA MSE=0.127, and GCN+Trans achieves Davis MSE=0.165/KIBA MSE=0.129.
The HGTDP-DTA model outperforms all listed baseline categories (ML-based, SQ-based, GR-based, FU-based) on the Davis dataset in terms of MSE and r_m^2 metrics.
The HGTDP-DTA model achieves best performance on MSE (0.119), CI (0.912), and Pearson (0.913) on the KIBA dataset, and second-best on r_m^2 (0.815) compared to all listed baselines.

t-SNE visualization of affinity representations is implemented, dividing affinities into weak and strong clusters using pKd=7 for Davis and KIBA score=12.1 for KIBA, and projecting learned representations before the final prediction layer into 2D space.
The t-SNE visualization results show that HGTDP-DTA can effectively distinguish weak affinities (red) from strong affinities (blue) in the representation space, consistent with Figure 4 in the paper.
